title: Toward Near-Real-Time Marine Oil Spill Detection in SAR Imagery using Quantum-Assisted SVM
abstract: Marine oil spills require rapid detection to mitigate severe ecological and economic damage. While satellite-based Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) provides essential all-weather monitoring, analyzing this data remains challenging. Deep learning models often require massive datasets and incur high latency. To address this, a pixel-wise quantum-assisted Support Vector Machine (QSVM) bagging ensemble is developed. Quantum annealing is leveraged to optimize the support vectors of individual weak SVMs on small data subsets, which are then classically aggregated.   The approach is evaluated on Sentinel-1 imagery using both quantum simulation and physical quantum annealing hardware. The quantum-assisted pipeline achieved performance comparable to a rigorous classical baseline, yielding an Intersection-over-Union (IoU) of 0.60 and a balanced accuracy of 0.89. Complementary experiments with gate-based quantum computing demonstrated similar segmentation accuracy, although the annealing approach offered superior inference efficiency. Generalization was further assessed on independent oil spill imagery from the Strait of Hormuz, demonstrating the potential transferability of the trained pipeline to geographically distinct spill events. These results establish the feasibility of quantum-assisted, segmentation pipelines for near-real-time environmental monitoring.
summary: This paper proposes a quantum-assisted machine learning pipeline for the rapid detection of oil spills in satellite radar imagery. By using quantum annealing to optimize SVMs, the researchers achieved high-accuracy segmentation with much faster inference speeds than traditional gate-based quantum approaches. The method proved robust and transferable to geographically different maritime regions.
main_problem: Achieving near-real-time, low-latency marine oil spill detection in SAR imagery while overcoming the high computational costs and data requirements of deep learning.
main_result: A quantum-assisted SVM bagging ensemble achieved performance (IoU 0.60, balanced accuracy 0.89) comparable to classical baselines, with annealing-based approaches offering superior inference efficiency over gate-based methods.
method: A pixel-wise bagging ensemble of SVMs where quantum annealing is used to optimize support vectors via a QUBO formulation, and gate-based quantum computing is used for quantum kernel methods.
model_or_system: The framework uses a pixel-wise bagging ensemble of 500 base learners applied to 5D feature vectors from Sentinel-1 SAR imagery, utilizing D-Wave quantum annealing hardware and gate-based quantum circuit simulations.
key_observables: Intersection-over-Union (IoU), F1-score, balanced accuracy, inference time, and training time.
important_parameters: 5D feature vectors (VV intensity, VH/VV ratio, local VV entropy, local VV standard deviation, and Sobel gradient magnitude); 256x256 image resolution.
main_assumptions: Assumes that small, randomly sampled subsets of data can effectively represent the full dataset through a bagging ensemble; notes the emulator-to-hardware gap due to noise and connectivity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a qualitative comparison of segmentation masks (Ground Truth, Classical, Annealing, and Gate-based) across different prediction qualities; Figure 2 shows qualitative segmentation results for an independent oil spill in the Strait of Hormuz.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the environmental problem and limitations of deep learning, proposes a quantum-assisted SVM ensemble, details the annealing and gate-based implementations, evaluates performance on Sentinel-1 data, compares computational trade-offs, and tests generalization on a geographically distinct dataset.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Qualitative comparison of segmentation performance across classical and quantum architectures. Top row: High-fidelity segmentation on an easily separable spill. Middle row: Intermediate performance exhibiting partial boundary degradation. Bottom row: Poor segmentation demonstrating susceptibility to complex speckle noise and look-alikes. Columns from left to right: SAR Image, Ground Truth Mask, Classical SVM prediction, QSVM (Annealing) prediction, and QSVM (Gate-Based) prediction.

Fig. 2. Strait of Hormuz oil spill segmentation results. Columns from left to right: SAR Image, Ground Truth Mask, Classical SVM prediction, QSVM (Annealing) prediction, and QSVM (Gate-Based) prediction.
